scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Plutarch writes about the contemporary reality where Greeks are subjects of the Roman Empire and excluded from imperial governance.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Plutarch wrote 'Precepts of Statecraft' around 100 CE, during the peak of his literary and public career.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text describes the specific administrative autonomy granted to Greek cities while under the authority of the Roman proconsul.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Plutarch's floruit around 100 CE, reflecting the administrative status of Greek cities in his time.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Plutarch discusses the civic duties of a contemporary Greek aristocrat (Menemachus) within the Roman provincial system.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The text is an address to a contemporary of Plutarch, written circa 100 CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The author addresses the governance of contemporary Athenian citizens subject to Roman proconsuls and 'the boots of Roman soldiers'.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Plutarch writes this advice to a contemporary politician around 100 CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Plutarch critiques the contemporary tendency of Greek city councils to refer all local decisions to Roman authorities.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This reflects the real-time political habits of Greek cities toward Rome during Plutarch's life, circa 100 CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: This describes the legal hierarchy where a Roman proconsul's edict overrides local Greek political decisions.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Plutarch's career around 100 CE, describing the contemporary relationship between Roman power and Greek local rule.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The author cites an anecdote about the Megarians granting citizenship to Alexander the Great to define the concept of citizenship.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The rule describes a specific historical action taken by Megara during the career of Alexander the Great, circa -330.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Plutarch uses historical examples to define citizenship and 'politeia' for his contemporary audience under Roman rule.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Stated as a contemporary political definition by Plutarch at his floruit circa 100 CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text explicitly discusses the oligarchy established by Lycurgus at Sparta and the power sharing among peers.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Though written later, it describes the traditional Spartan constitution viewed as an operative historical model; -348 is a representative classical date.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Demosthenes describes a specific legislative action taken by a member of the Athenian senate.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: This refers to the legal procedures for state funds and war taxes (eisphora) in 4th-century Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: These statutes were invoked in a trial occurring around -340.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule describes 'atimia' (disfranchisement) for debtors to the Athenian treasury.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This is a contemporary legal consequence described in Demosthenes’ speech around -340.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text explicitly cites an Athenian statute regarding marriage between aliens and citizens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This law was in effect and cited during the prosecution of Neaera around -340.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The speaker describes the purchase and ownership of individuals as chattel within the Athenian legal context.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the contemporary Athenian institution of slavery described in court around -340.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text refers to the legal requirement for aliens (metics) to have an Athenian 'prostates' (patron).
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This was a standard legal requirement for metics in Athens during the mid-4th century BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text describes the specific Athenian legal procedure of 'aphairesis eis eleutherian' for claiming freedom.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This Athenian legal remedy was being actively utilized in the case described circa -340.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text cites an Athenian law regarding the return of the 'proix' (dowry) upon divorce.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This represents a contemporary civil law of the orators' period around -340.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text describes Athenian penalties for misrepresenting the status of a bride in a citizen marriage.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text outlines the requirements for the Basilissa, a central religious office in the Athenian state religion.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule describes the exclusion of certain women from Athenian public sacrifices and festivals.
